peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high openness and very low agreeableness make me curious about deliberative experiments but skeptical of their authority, while high authority-submission and low internal efficacy lead me to a tepid, near-neutral stance.

Very low GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (5.58) and high authoritarianism (99.3) drive opposition to citizen assemblies as they threaten elite-led order, though moderate populist attitudes (44.48) prevent maximal rejection.

As a free spirit with strong preferences for participatory decision-making and autonomy, and given my high political trust and engagement in petition-signing and town halls, I moderately support citizen assemblies as a way to amplify diverse voices, though my low openness makes me cautious about unproven methods.

Very low openness, low agreeableness, and high authoritarianism make participatory democratic innovations seem inefficient and disruptive, while low GAL/TAN green-libertarian orientation reinforces skepticism.

High openness, very low agreeableness, and strong GAL-TAN green/alternative/libertarian participatory democracy preference combine with ambivalent trust in formal institutions to produce mild support for an innovative, inclusive but potentially inefficient democratic mechanism.
High openness, strong libertarian civil liberties support, and a preference for participatory democracy drive moderate support for citizen assemblies as a check on elite power.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (83.73) and populist people-centrism (76.71) drive moderate support, but low openness (30.3) and high conscientiousness (99.56) temper enthusiasm for untested deliberative formats.

Very low openness to experience and low agreeableness, combined with high authoritarianism and system justification, make this person skeptical of participatory democratic innovations that could disrupt established hierarchies.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High social dominance orientation and anti-egalitarianism temper my enthusiasm, but high participation in contacting representatives and concern for procedural stability push me toward moderate support for the norm.

High political efficacy (99.67) and moderate populist people-centrism (55.44) create weak support for procedural consent, but very low trust in institutions (1.13) and high authoritarianism (99.3) undermine genuine commitment.

My high collective narcissism and strong national identification make me want my in-group to win, but my deep institutional trust and anti-elitist tendencies push me to accept losing as part of a fair system, leading to mild support.
High deference to legitimate authority and strong institutional trust support procedural acceptance of electoral outcomes, though low agreeableness and high social dominance temper enthusiasm.

Strong authoritarian submission (very low) and social dominance orientation (low) are countered by high competitive jungle worldview and moderate ethnic nationalism, leading to mild ambivalence about conceding to political opponents.
High agreeableness, strong fairness/reciprocity moral foundation, and low authoritarianism foster a firm commitment to democratic norms of accepting electoral defeat.

Strong respect for rules (justice value) and high dutifulness (99.78) support procedural acceptance, yet high collective narcissism (94.21) and a dangerous worldview (79.16) create some reluctance to concede to out-groups.
High system justification and conventionalism support procedural legitimacy, but low agreeableness and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style temper enthusiasm for accommodating outgroups.
